---
title: "Introduction to DECODE"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Introduction to DECODE}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    collapse = TRUE,
    comment = "#>",
    fig.align = "center",
    out.width = "100%"
)
# rmarkdown::render("vignettes_prompt/DECODE.Rmd")
```

```{r setup}
r_dir <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    "/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/R"
r_files <- list.files( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    r_dir,
    pattern = "[.]r$|[.]R$", full.names = TRUE
)
invisible(lapply(r_files, source)) # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
# library(DECODE) # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
set.seed(1)
```

DECODE (**De**ciphering **C**ancer **O**rigin from **D**NA **E**volution) decomposes mutations from a bulk DNA-sequencing sample
into a neutral tail and mutation clusters, based on their Variant Allele Frequencies (VAFs).
The algorithm corrects for sample-specific sequencing coverage and mutation-calling biases when computing the expected Site Frequency Spectrum (SFS).

This vignette walks through a complete hands-on workflow:

- Section 1:  Prepare a mutation table with annotated copy numbers
- Section 2:  Decompose diploid mutations with DECODE
- Section 3:  Generate diagnostic plots
- Section 4:  Extract key biological parameters
- Section 5:  Assign mutations to DECODE components

# 1. Prepare a mutation table with annotated copy numbers

DECODE requires a mutation table with columns `Ref_count` and `Alt_count`,
containing the reference and alternative read counts for each mutation, respectively.

```{r show-mutation-table}
# mutation_table <- read.csv( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "mutation_table.csv", package = "DECODE")
# )
mutation_table <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    read.csv("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table.csv")
head(mutation_table)
```

NOTE FOR YANJIE: INCLUDE COLUMN Mutation_ID AS 1ST COLUMN.



Copy-number segments define the major and minor allele copy number along each chromosome.
DECODE is run on mutations in diploid regions (`nMajor = 1`, `nMinor = 1`), where each mutation lies on a single haploid copy and $\mathrm{VAF} \approx \mathrm{CCF}/2$ (see [Section 7c](#7c-purity-and-cancer-cell-fraction-ccf)).

The example file uses tab-separated values with the following columns:

```{r show-cn-segments}
# cn_segments <- read.delim( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "copy_number_segments.txt", package = "DECODE")
# )
cn_segments <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    read.delim("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/copy_number_segments.txt")
head(cn_segments)
```

Required columns:

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `chr` | Chromosome identifier (e.g. `1`, `2`, …, `X`) |
| `startpos` | Start coordinate of the segment (bp) |
| `endpos` | End coordinate of the segment (bp) |
| `nMajor` | Copy number of the major allele |
| `nMinor` | Copy number of the minor allele |

Segments on the same chromosome must not overlap, and should be sorted by `startpos`.

**Note on chromosome naming.**
Mutation tables often use a `chr` prefix (`chr1`), while copy-number files may use bare chromosome names (`1`).
We normalize chromosome labels in the next section before matching.

## 1c. Annotate mutations with copy number

We assign each mutation to the copy-number segment that contains its genomic position.
For each chromosome, `findInterval()` locates the segment whose `startpos` is less than or equal to the mutation position.

```{r annotate-cn-function}
annotate_mutations_with_cn <- function(mutation_table, cn_segments) {
    if (!all(c("Chromosome", "Position") %in% colnames(mutation_table))) {
        stop("mutation_table must contain columns 'Chromosome' and 'Position'.")
    }
    if (!all(c("chr", "startpos", "endpos", "nMajor", "nMinor") %in% colnames(cn_segments))) {
        stop("cn_segments must contain columns 'chr', 'startpos', 'endpos', 'nMajor', and 'nMinor'.")
    }

    mutation_table <- mutation_table
    mutation_table$chr <- sub("^chr", "", as.character(mutation_table$Chromosome))
    cn_segments <- cn_segments
    cn_segments$chr <- as.character(cn_segments$chr)

    mutation_table$nMajor <- NA_integer_
    mutation_table$nMinor <- NA_integer_

    for (chr in unique(mutation_table$chr)) {
        mut_idx <- which(mutation_table$chr == chr)
        cn_chr <- cn_segments[cn_segments$chr == chr, , drop = FALSE]
        if (nrow(cn_chr) == 0) next

        cn_chr <- cn_chr[order(cn_chr$startpos), , drop = FALSE]
        pos <- mutation_table$Position[mut_idx]
        seg_idx <- findInterval(pos, cn_chr$startpos)

        # Keep only positions that fall inside the matched segment
        valid <- seg_idx > 0
        valid[valid] <- pos[valid] <= cn_chr$endpos[seg_idx[valid]]
        mutation_table$nMajor[mut_idx[valid]] <- cn_chr$nMajor[seg_idx[valid]]
        mutation_table$nMinor[mut_idx[valid]] <- cn_chr$nMinor[seg_idx[valid]]
    }

    return(mutation_table)
}
```

Apply the annotation:

```{r annotate-cn}
mutation_table_cn <- annotate_mutations_with_cn(
    mutation_table = mutation_table,
    cn_segments = cn_segments
)

cat(
    "Total mutations:", nrow(mutation_table_cn), "\n",
    "Mapped to a CN segment:", sum(!is.na(mutation_table_cn$nMajor)), "\n"
)
head(mutation_table_cn[, c("Chromosome", "Position", "Ref_count", "Alt_count", "nMajor", "nMinor")])
```

Mutations with missing copy-number values lie outside the supplied segment file (for example, on chromosomes not present in the CN table) and should be removed before filtering.

```{r remove-unmapped}
mutation_table_cn <- mutation_table_cn[!is.na(mutation_table_cn$nMajor), ]
nrow(mutation_table_cn)
```

# 2. Decompose diploid mutations with DECODE

## 2a. Filter to diploid mutations

DECODE models clonal structure from VAF under the assumption that each mutation occurs on a single haploid copy in a diploid locus.
Therefore, retain only mutations with `nMajor == 1` and `nMinor == 1`:

```{r filter-diploid}
mutation_table_diploid <- mutation_table_cn[
    mutation_table_cn$nMajor == 1 & mutation_table_cn$nMinor == 1,
]

cat(
    "Diploid mutations (nMajor = 1, nMinor = 1):", nrow(mutation_table_diploid), "\n"
)
```

Remove mutations with zero reference or alternate read counts, and keep only the columns required by `DECODE()`:

```{r prepare-decode-input}
mutation_table_decode <- mutation_table_diploid[
    mutation_table_diploid$Ref_count > 0 & mutation_table_diploid$Alt_count > 0,
    c("Ref_count", "Alt_count")
]

head(mutation_table_decode)
nrow(mutation_table_decode)
```

DECODE needs a sufficient number of mutations to estimate the SFS reliably.
If fewer than roughly 100 mutations remain after filtering, consider relaxing upstream filters or checking copy-number inference quality.
The diploid assumption used here is the same as in [Section 7c](#7c-purity-and-cancer-cell-fraction-ccf) when interpreting cluster frequencies as CCF.

## 2b. Run DECODE

The main function is `DECODE()`.
It accepts the filtered mutation table and returns a list object with model-selection results, best-fit parameters, and SFS summaries.

Key arguments include:

- `sample_id`: label for the sample.
- `mutation_table`: data frame with `Ref_count` and `Alt_count`.
- `neutral_tail`: whether to include a neutral tail (`NA` = compare with and without tail automatically).
- `N_clusters_min`, `N_clusters_max`: range of cluster counts to evaluate.
- `ABCSMCDRF_nParticles`: number of ABC-SMC-DRF particles per iteration (larger values improve accuracy but increase runtime).

```{r run-decode, cache=TRUE}
DECODE_result <- DECODE(
    sample_id = "Example",
    mutation_table = mutation_table_decode
)
```

The console output reports the selected model (with or without neutral tail), the optimal number of clusters, and parameter estimates for each component.

Inspect the main result fields:

```{r inspect-result}
names(DECODE_result)
DECODE_result$best_with_tail
DECODE_result$best_N_clusters
```

Parameter extraction from the best-fitting model is covered in [Section 7](#7-extract-key-parameters-from-decode-results).

The example below uses default `ABCSMCDRF_nParticles = rep(1000, 10)` and `N_clusters_max = 3`.
For production analyses, consider a wider `N_clusters_max`; to speed up exploratory runs, reduce `ABCSMCDRF_nParticles`.

## 2c. Save the DECODE result

Save the full result object as an `.rds` file so you can reload it later without rerunning inference:

```{r save-result}
result_file <- paste0("DECODE.rds")
saveRDS(DECODE_result, file = result_file)
result_file
```

Reload a saved result in a new R session and skip Sections 3–4:

```{r load-result, eval=FALSE}
DECODE_result <- readRDS(result_file)
```

# 3. Generate diagnostic plots

DECODE provides several plotting functions in `DECODE_plots.r`:

- `DECODE_plot_SFS()`: observed vs. fitted SFS for the best model.
- `DECODE_plot_model_selection()`: comparison of candidate models (with/without tail, different cluster counts).
- `DECODE_plot_criteria()`: model-selection criteria across candidates.
- `DECODE_plot_readcounts()`: mutation read-count distribution with DECODE filtering thresholds.

Both `DECODE_plot_SFS()` and `DECODE_plot_model_selection()` accept optional `filename`, `width`, `height`, `units`, and `res` arguments.
Their defaults are `width = 30`, `height = 15`, `units = "in"`, and `res = 150` (i.e. 4500 × 2250 pixels when saved as PNG).
The plot layout and font sizes in `DECODE_plots.r` are calibrated for this canvas size.

When embedding plots in an HTML vignette, save the PNG first and then display it with `knitr::include_graphics()`.
Re-drawing the ggplot or grid object inside a small default knitr device (~7 × 7 inches) compresses the same absolute font sizes into a much smaller figure and causes clipping.
The workflow below writes PNG files to `DECODE_results/` in a hidden chunk, shows the plotting code in the visible chunks, and embeds the saved PNG files so the HTML output matches the on-disk figures.

```{r plot-paths, include=FALSE}
sfs_file <- "DECODE_SFS"
model_selection_file <- "DECODE_model_selection"
```

```{r plot-save, include=FALSE}
DECODE_plot_SFS(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = sfs_file,
    filetype = "png"
)
DECODE_plot_model_selection(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = model_selection_file,
    filetype = "png"
)
```

## 3a. Site Frequency Spectrum plot

```{r plot-sfs-code, eval=FALSE}
sfs_file <- "DECODE_SFS"

DECODE_plot_SFS(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = sfs_file,
    filetype = "png"
)
```

```{r plot-sfs-fig, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(sfs_file, ".png"))
```

The PNG is saved to `DECODE_results/DECODE_SFS_example_sample.png` at 30 × 15 inches and 150 dpi.

## 3b. Model selection plot

```{r plot-model-selection-code, eval=FALSE}
model_selection_file <- "DECODE_model_selection"

DECODE_plot_model_selection(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = model_selection_file,
    filetype = "png"
)
```

```{r plot-model-selection-fig, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(model_selection_file, ".png"))
```

## 3c. Optional diagnostic plots

`DECODE_plot_criteria()` and `DECODE_plot_readcounts()` summarize model selection and the read-count filtering thresholds used by DECODE.
They return ggplot objects and can be saved with `ggsave()` or displayed with `print()`.

```{r plot-optional, eval=FALSE}
print(DECODE_plot_criteria(DECODE_result = DECODE_result))
print(DECODE_plot_readcounts(DECODE_result = DECODE_result))
```

# 4. Extract key biological parameters

After DECODE finishes, the selected model is stored in `DECODE_result`.
The ABC-SMC-DRF algorithm returns a **posterior sample** of parameters in `best_fit$parameters` (one row per particle).
In practice, each scalar parameter is summarized by its **posterior mean**.

## 4a. Retrieve the best fit

First locate the best-fitting model chosen by DECODE (with or without neutral tail, and the optimal cluster count):

```{r extract-best-fit}
if (DECODE_result$best_with_tail) {
    fit_key <- "fits_with_tail"
} else {
    fit_key <- "fits_without_tail"
}

best_fit <- DECODE_result[[fit_key]]$all_fits[[
    paste0(DECODE_result$best_N_clusters, "_clusters")
]]
parameters <- best_fit$parameters

DECODE_result$best_with_tail
DECODE_result$best_N_clusters
```

## 4b. Number of clusters

The selected number of mutation clusters is stored directly in the result object:

```{r extract-n-clusters}
N_clusters <- DECODE_result$best_N_clusters
N_clusters
```

## 4c. Purity and Cancer Cell Fraction (CCF) {#7c-purity-and-cancer-cell-fraction-ccf}

DECODE estimates a cluster frequency `Cluster_i_freq` (denoted `f` in the console report) for each cluster `i`.
Under the **diploid assumption** used in this vignette (`nMajor = 1`, `nMinor = 1`), each cluster mutation lies on a single haploid copy, so for every diploid cluster

$$\mathrm{VAF} \approx \frac{\mathrm{CCF}_i}{2}$$

Equivalently, for cluster `i` we approximate

$$\mathrm{CCF}_i \approx \min\left(1,\ 2 \times \mathrm{mean}(\texttt{Cluster_i_freq})\right)$$

where `Cluster_i_freq` is the posterior sample of `f` for cluster `i`.

The cluster with the **highest** posterior mean `Cluster_i_freq` is interpreted as the descendant of the tumour **most recent common ancestor (MRCA)**.
Its CCF equals the tumour cell fraction in the sample, i.e. **purity**:

$$\mathrm{purity} \approx \mathrm{CCF}_{i^*}, \quad i^* = \arg\max_i \mathrm{mean}(\texttt{Cluster_i_freq})$$

```{r extract-ccf-purity}
cluster_mean_freq <- vapply(
    seq_len(N_clusters),
    function(i) mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_freq")]]),
    numeric(1)
)
names(cluster_mean_freq) <- paste0("Cluster_", seq_len(N_clusters))

cluster_ccf <- pmin(1, 2 * cluster_mean_freq)
names(cluster_ccf) <- names(cluster_mean_freq)

mrca_cluster <- names(which.max(cluster_mean_freq))
purity <- cluster_ccf[mrca_cluster]

data.frame(
    cluster = names(cluster_mean_freq),
    mean_VAF = cluster_mean_freq,
    CCF = cluster_ccf
)
purity
mrca_cluster
```

The MRCA-descendant cluster is `mrca_cluster`; its CCF equals `purity`.

## 4d. Mutation burden per cluster {#7d-mutation-burden-per-cluster}

DECODE does **not** use every diploid mutation when fitting the SFS.
During the automatic read-count filtering step (`choose_mutation_thresholds()`), three non-overlapping subsets are defined:

| Subset | Typical use | Mutation-count column |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| `inference_A` | Primary inference (most mutations retained) | `Cluster_i_Nmut_A` |
| `inference_B` | Secondary inference (stricter thresholds) | `Cluster_i_Nmut_B` |
| `validation` | Held-out validation SFS (strictest thresholds) | `Cluster_i_Nmut_validation` |

For a **quick start**, we recommend summarizing mutation burden with the **inference A** counts:

```{r extract-mutation-burden}
cluster_burden <- data.frame(
    cluster = paste0("Cluster_", seq_len(N_clusters)),
    Nmut_A = vapply(
        seq_len(N_clusters),
        function(i) mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_A")]]),
        numeric(1)
    ),
    Nmut_B = vapply(
        seq_len(N_clusters),
        function(i) mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_B")]]),
        numeric(1)
    ),
    Nmut_validation = vapply(
        seq_len(N_clusters),
        function(i) mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_validation")]]),
        numeric(1)
    ),
    CCF = cluster_ccf
)
cluster_burden
```

If the selected model includes a neutral tail (`DECODE_result$best_with_tail == TRUE`), the tail mutation burden can be read similarly, for example `mean(parameters$Tail_Nmut_A)`.

## 4e. Summary table

Combine the key quantities into one table:

```{r extract-summary}
decode_summary <- data.frame(
    sample_id = DECODE_result$sample_id,
    neutral_tail = DECODE_result$best_with_tail,
    N_clusters = N_clusters,
    purity = purity,
    mrca_cluster = mrca_cluster
)
decode_summary
cluster_burden[, c("cluster", "CCF", "Nmut_A")]
```

# 5. Mutation assignment

DECODE fits the SFS at the **bulk** level.
To associate individual mutations with the inferred neutral tail or mutation clusters, use `DECODE_mutation_assignment()` in `DECODE_analysis.r`.

This function requires a mutation table with a `normalized_VAF` column: the VAF each mutation would have if it occurred in a **diploid** locus, on a **single** mutant copy, in the same sample purity.
Mutations can come from **any** copy-number background (not only `nMajor = 1`, `nMinor = 1`), as long as `normalized_VAF` is computed correctly.

## 5a. Normalized VAF

Let $f_i$ be the observed VAF of mutation $i$, with multiplicity $m_i$ and total copy number $c_i = \mathrm{major\_cn} + \mathrm{minor\_cn}$ in a sample with purity $\rho$.
Following Tarabichi et al. (2021), the **normalized VAF** is the expected VAF if the mutation were diploid:

$$\bar{f}_i = f_i \cdot \frac{\dfrac{\rho}{2\rho + 2(1-\rho)}}{\dfrac{m_i \cdot \rho}{c_i \cdot \rho + 2(1-\rho)}}$$

For **diploid** mutations (`major_cn = 1`, `minor_cn = 1`, `multiplicity = 1`), this simplifies to $\bar{f}_i = f_i$.

Use the purity estimate from [Section 7c](#7c-purity-and-cancer-cell-fraction-ccf) as $\rho$.

```{r normalized-vaf-function}
compute_normalized_vaf <- function(VAF, multiplicity, total_cn, purity) {
    vaf_if_diploid <- purity / (2 * purity + 2 * (1 - purity))
    vaf_expected <- (multiplicity * purity) / (total_cn * purity + 2 * (1 - purity))
    VAF * vaf_if_diploid / vaf_expected
}
```

## 5b. Mutation multiplicity

The normalized VAF formula requires the **multiplicity** $m_i$: the number of mutant copies of the alternate allele in the tumor cells.

When `major_cn <= 1`, only one mutant copy can be present on the major allele, so $m_i = 1$.

When `major_cn > 1`, multiple mutant copies are possible.
We recommend estimating multiplicity with **[GRITIC](https://github.com/VanLoo-lab/gritic)**, which takes:

- a mutation table (`Chromosome`, `Position`, `Tumor_Ref_Count`, `Tumor_Alt_Count`),
- a copy-number table (`Chromosome`, `Segment_Start`, `Segment_End`, `Major_CN`, `Minor_CN`), and
- the sample purity $\rho$.

No further inputs are required. Install GRITIC with `pip install gritic` and run, for example:

```{r gritic-example, eval=FALSE}
# Convert DECODE inputs to GRITIC format, then run (command-line example):
# python rungritic_cmd.py \
#   --mutation_table path/to/snv_table.tsv \
#   --copy_number_table path/to/cn_table.tsv \
#   --purity 0.87 \
#   --sample_id example_sample \
#   --output path/to/gritic_output
#
# GRITIC writes a processed mutation table with multiplicity probabilities
# (_mutation_table.tsv). Use the estimated multiplicity for each SNV.
```

In this vignette, the example file `mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv` supplies multiplicity for all 100 SNVs (including loci with `major_cn > 1`).
For your own data, run GRITIC and merge its multiplicity estimates into your annotated mutation table by `Chromosome` and `Position`.

## 5c. Build the mutation assignment table

The worked example below reads `mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv` directly.
It contains 100 SNVs with copy number and multiplicity already annotated (including non-diploid loci).
For a full cohort, start from `mutation_table_cn` (Section 2), add a `multiplicity` column (`1` when `nMajor <= 1`; GRITIC otherwise), then compute `normalized_VAF`.

```{r build-assignment-table}
# mutation_assignment_input <- read.csv( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv", package = "DECODE")
# )
mutation_assignment_input <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    read.csv("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv")

mutation_assignment_input$VAF <- with(
    mutation_assignment_input,
    Alt_count / (Ref_count + Alt_count)
)
mutation_assignment_input$total_cn <- with(
    mutation_assignment_input,
    major_cn + minor_cn
)

# Recompute normalized VAF using purity from DECODE (Section 7)
mutation_assignment_input$normalized_VAF <- compute_normalized_vaf(
    VAF = mutation_assignment_input$VAF,
    multiplicity = mutation_assignment_input$multiplicity,
    total_cn = mutation_assignment_input$total_cn,
    purity = purity
)

head(
    mutation_assignment_input[
        ,
        c(
            "Chromosome", "Position", "Ref_count", "Alt_count",
            "major_cn", "minor_cn", "multiplicity", "VAF", "normalized_VAF"
        )
    ]
)
```

For loci with `major_cn <= 1`, set `multiplicity <- 1`.
For loci with `major_cn > 1`, populate `multiplicity` from GRITIC output before calling `compute_normalized_vaf()`.

## 5d. Soft assignment with `DECODE_mutation_assignment()`

`DECODE_mutation_assignment()` maps each mutation to the VAF bin of its `normalized_VAF` in the fitted SFS, then reads off the **component proportions** in that bin.
This yields a soft assignment: columns `prob_tail`, `prob_cluster_1`, `prob_cluster_2`, … give the probability that each mutation belongs to each DECODE component.

By default the function uses the **best overall fit** (`neutral_tail = NA`, `N_clusters = NULL`) and the **inference A** SFS (`mode = "inference_A"`).
Other modes (`inference_B`, `validation`) can be selected to match the mutation subset used in [Section 7d](#7d-mutation-burden-per-cluster).

```{r mutation-assignment}
mutation_assignment <- DECODE_mutation_assignment(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    mutation_table = mutation_assignment_input,
    mode = "inference_A"
)

prob_cols <- grep("^prob_", names(mutation_assignment), value = TRUE)
head(mutation_assignment[, c("Chromosome", "Position", "normalized_VAF", prob_cols)])
```

Each row's probability columns sum to 1 (when the mutation falls inside a valid SFS bin).
For example, a mutation with high `prob_cluster_1` and low `prob_tail` is likely assigned to cluster 1 rather than the neutral tail.

Save the full assignment table:

```{r save-assignment}
assignment_file <- "mutation_assignment.csv"
write.csv(mutation_assignment, file = assignment_file, row.names = FALSE)
assignment_file
```
